{{Short description|Conceptual tool in sociology of education}}
'''Science capital''' is a conceptual tool developed by [[Louise Archer (academic)|Louise Archer]] and colleagues at [[King's College London]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Archer |first1=Louise |last2=Dawson |first2=Emily |last3=DeWitt |first3=Jennifer |last4=Seakins |first4=Amy |last5=Wong |first5=Billy |title=''Science Capital'': A Conceptual, Methodological, and Empirical Argument for Extending Bourdieusian Notions of Capital Beyond the Arts |journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching |date=2015 |volume=52 |issue=7 |page=992-948 |doi=10.1002/tea.21227 |bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A |doi-access=free }}</ref> It uses the theoretical frameworks created by French sociologist [[Pierre Bourdieu]] to summarise an individual’s science-related [[habitus (sociology)|habitus]] and [[cultural capital|capital]]. It can be used to help understanding how [[social class]] affects people's aspirations and involvement in science. The concept comes from research in education but is also used more broadly in practice and policy, for instance in the work of the [[Science and Technology Select Committee|Science and Technology Committee]] of the House of Commons in the UK.<ref name = HoC>{{Cite web|url=https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/cm201617/cmselect/cmsctech/162/162.pdf|title=House of Commons Science and Technology Committee Science communication and engagement|last=House of Commons|access-date=8 May 2017}}</ref>

==Definition==
Science capital can be defined as the sum of all the science-related knowledge, attitudes, experiences and resources that an individual builds up  through their life. This includes what science they know about, what they think about science, the people they know who have an understanding of science, and the day-to-day engagement they have with science.<ref name = HoC/>{{rp|10}}

Science capital is made up of science related [[Cultural capital|cultural]] and [[social capital]] (institutionalized and/or embodied through knowledge, consumption, credentials, and social networks) as well as [[Habitus (sociology)|habitus]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Dawson|first2=Emily|last3=DeWitt|first3=Jen|last4=Seakins|first4=Amy|last5=Wong|first5=Billy|date=2015|title=Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts|url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1469306/10/Dawson_1469306.pdf|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=52|issue=7|pages=922–948|doi=10.1002/tea.21227|bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=DeWitt|first2=Jen|last3=Willis|first3=Beatrice|date=2013|title=Adolescent boys' science aspirations: Masculinity, capital, and power|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=51 |issue=1|pages=1–30|doi=10.1002/tea.21122}}</ref> Researchers have suggested that science capital does not exist in isolation but has its value determined by someone's wider context and environment.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Dawson|first2=Emily|last3=DeWitt|first3=Jen|last4=Seakins|first4=Amy|last5=Wong|first5=Billy|date=2015|title="Science capital": A conceptual, methodological, and empirical argument for extending bourdieusian notions of capital beyond the arts|url=https://centaur.reading.ac.uk/69983/1/Archer_et_al-2015-Journal_of_Research_in_Science_Teaching.pdf|journal=Journal of Research in Science Teaching|volume=52|issue=7|pages=922–948|doi=10.1002/tea.21227|bibcode=2015JRScT..52..922A|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Science capital has been framed around eight key dimensions,<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=http://www.ecsite.eu/activities-and-services/news-and-publications/digital-spokes/issue-25#section=section-indepth&href=/feature/depth/science-capital|title=Science capital|last1=Seakins|first1=Amy|last2=King|first2=Heather|date=2016|website=Spokes|publisher=Ecsite|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref> drawing on statistical analysis of survey data from UK school students:<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=DeWitt|first1=Jen|last2=Archer|first2=Louise|last3=Mau|first3=Ada|date=2016|title=Dimensions of science capital: exploring its potential for understanding students' science participation|url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/10042819/1/DeWitt_Dimensions_science_capital.pdf|journal=International Journal of Science Education|volume=38 |issue=16|pages=2431–2449|doi=10.1080/09500693.2016.1248520|bibcode=2016IJSEd..38.2431D|s2cid=152260309}}</ref>
# [[Scientific literacy]] 
# Science-related attitudes, values and dispositions
# Knowledge about the transferability of science (that science 'open doors' to many careers)
# Science media consumption
# Participation in out-of-school science learning contexts
# Family science skills, knowledge and qualifications
# Knowing people in science-related roles
# Talking about science in everyday life&nbsp;
These eight dimensions collapse into four 'types' of science capital: what you know ([[scientific literacy]]); how you think (attitudes and dispositions); what you do (science-related activities and behaviours); and who you know (social contacts and networks). The first three 'types' include [[Habitus (sociology)|habitus]] and [[cultural capital]] and the fourth, [[social capital]].<ref name=":3" /> Research shows that measuring science capital provides a better prediction of science aspirations than a general measure of capital.<ref name=":3" />&nbsp;

==History==

The concept of science capital draws on [[Pierre Bourdieu]]’s work about capital and [[social reproduction]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Distinction: A social critique of the judgement of taste|last=Bourdieu|first=Pierre|publisher=Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd|year=1984|location=London|translator-last=Nice|translator-first=Richard}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|title=Reproduction in education, society and culture|last1=Bourdieu|first1=Pierre|last2=Passeron|first2=Jean-Claude|publisher=Sage|year=1990|location=London}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|title=An invitation to reflexive sociology|last1=Bourdieu|first1=Pierre|last2=Wacquant|first2=Loic J. D.|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=1992|location=Chicago}}</ref> Science capital builds on, but is distinct from, how [[Pierre Bourdieu]] used the terms scientific, technical or technological capital.<ref>{{Cite book|title=[[Science of Science and Reflexivity]]|last=Bourdieu|first=Pierre|publisher=University of Chicago Press and Polity Press|year=2004|location=Cambridge}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|title=The social structures of the economy|last=Bourdieu|first=Pierre|publisher=Polity|year=2005|isbn=978-0-7456-2539-3|location=Cambridge and Malden, MA.}}</ref> Science Capital is not a new or separate form of capital. Instead, science capital is a way to think about grouping different kinds of science-related social and [[cultural capital]], particularly those that people could use or exchange to support their attainment, engagement and/or participation in science.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

Science capital was first developed by Louise Archer and colleagues in the ASPIRES<ref name=":0" /> project. Building on five years of research with youths aged 10–14 and their families, ASPIRES found that children from families with more science related-resources (such as parents with scientific hobbies or careers) were more likely to want to pursue science at school and as a career. The concept of science capital was developed as a way to understand why these science-related resources, attitudes and aspirations led some children to pursue science, while others did not.

Science capital was developed conceptually and empirically through the Enterprising Science project and the ASPIRES 2 project. The Enterprising Science project developed a survey to measure science capital and extended the concept of science capital beyond homes and into schools and museums.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/education/education-news/exclusive-science-careers-favour-rich-male-asians-according-to-research-9799304.html|title=Science careers favour rich, male Asians, according to research|last=Garner|first=Richard|date=16 October 2014|work=The Independent|access-date=7 May 2017}}</ref> Science capital is being used to develop strategies for teaching in primary and secondary schools<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.kcl.ac.uk/sspp/departments/education/research/Research-Centres/cppr/Research/currentpro/Enterprising-Science/Science-Capital-in-the-Classroom.aspx|title=Watch: Science Capital in the Classroom|last=King's College London|access-date=8 May 2017}}{{Dead link|date=October 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}</ref> and to develop measures of science capital for adults.

==ASPIRES==
ASPIRES, currently based at [[UCL Institute of Education]], is a 10-year longitudinal research project studying young people’s science and career aspirations.<ref>{{cite web|title=ASPIRES 2|url=http://www.ucl.ac.uk/ioe/departments-centres/departments/education-practice-and-society/aspires|website=UCL|access-date=19 March 2018|archive-date=13 December 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171213041733/http://www.ucl.ac.uk/ioe/departments-centres/departments/education-practice-and-society/aspires|url-status=dead}}</ref> The first ASPIRES study (2009-2013) tracked young people's science and career aspirations from age 10–14. ASPIRES 2  continues to track young people until age 19, to understand the changing influences of the family, school, careers education and social identities and inequalities on young people's science and career aspirations. Key findings include:

# Students with low Science Capital are unlikely to see science as ‘for me’. In the first phase of our project, we introduced the term Science Capital to refer to someone’s science-related qualifications, understanding, knowledge (about science and ‘how it works’), interest and social contacts (e.g. knowing someone who works in a science-related job). 
# Enjoyment of Science doesn’t translate into science aspirations.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Archer|first1=L.|last2=DeWitt|first2=J.|last3=Osbourne|first3=J.F.|last4=Dillon|first4=J.S.|last5=Wong|first5=B.|last6=Willis|first6=B.|title=ASPIRES: Young people's science and career aspirations, age 10-14|url=https://kclpure.kcl.ac.uk/portal/files/64130521/ASPIRES_Report_2013.pdf|website=King's Research Portal|publisher=King's College London|access-date=19 March 2018}}</ref>
# Current careers education is not just ‘patchy’ but patterned, particularly in terms of social inequalities.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Archer|first1=L.|last2=Moote|first2=J.K.|title=ASPIRES 2 Project Spotlight: Year 11 Students' Views of Careers Education and Work Experience|url=https://kclpure.kcl.ac.uk/portal/files/64130618/ASPIRES_2_Project_Spotlight_1.pdf|website=King's Research Portal|publisher=King's College London|access-date=19 March 2018}}</ref>
# The stratification of science at [[Key Stage 4]] may be contributing to the STEM skills gap.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Moote|first2=Julie|last3=Francis|first3=Becky|last4=DeWitt|first4=Jennifer|last5=Yeomans|first5=Lucy|title=Stratifying science: a Bourdieusian analysis of student views and experiences of school selective practices in relation to 'Triple Science' at KS4 in England|journal=Research Papers in Education|date=2016|volume=32|issue=3|pages=296–315|doi=10.1080/02671522.2016.1219382|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=MacLeod|first1=Emily|title=Is GCSE Triple Science making the STEM skills gap wider?|url=http://blogs.ucl.ac.uk/aspires/2016/04/21/is-gcse-triple-science-making-the-stem-skills-gap-wider/|website=ASPIRES 2: Project Blog|publisher=UCL|access-date=19 March 2018}}</ref>
# Girls pursuing the physical sciences post-16 are exceptional.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Archer|first1=Louise|last2=Moote|first2=Julie|last3=Francis|first3=Becky|last4=DeWitt|first4=Jennifer|last5=Yeomans|first5=Lucy|title=The "Exceptional" Physics Girl: A Sociological Analysis of Multimethod Data From Young Women Aged 10–16 to Explore Gendered Patterns of Post-16 Participation|journal=American Educational Research Journal|date=2017|volume=54|issue=1|pages=88–126|doi=10.3102/0002831216678379|s2cid=151449361|url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1546943/}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=MacLeod|first1=Emily|title=What makes the girls taking Physics A level so exceptional?|url=http://blogs.ucl.ac.uk/aspires/2016/01/15/what-makes-the-girls-taking-physics-a-level-so-exceptional/|website=ASPIRES 2: Project Blog|publisher=UCL|access-date=19 March 2018}}</ref>

This project was first based at King’s College London, having moved to the UCL Institute of Education in March 2017. It is funded by the [[Economic and Social Research Council]].

==Science capital in practice==

Science capital is used across a variety of educational settings to support science learning, particularly for children.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.newstatesman.com/sci-tech/2014/10/britain-needs-stop-discouraging-women-choosing-engineering-career|title=Britain needs to stop discouraging women from choosing engineering as a career|last=Rutherford|first=Fiona|date=2 October 2014|work=New Statesman|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://wellcome.ac.uk/sites/default/files/science-education-tracker-wellcome-feb16.pdf|title=Wellcome Trust: SET Development 2016|last=Wellcome Trust|date=February 2016|website=Wellcome Trust|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref> For instance, one British school aims to help students to develop science capital through taking part in science clubs,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.teachwire.net/news/encouraging-girls-black-pupils-to-enter-science-profession|title=Encouraging Girls, Black Pupils And Kids From Lower Socio-Economic Backgrounds To Enter The Sciences|last=Kenrick|first=Carole|date=12 January 2017|website=TeachWire|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref> while on a larger scale, the [[Science Museum Group]] uses science capital as a concept to inform their strategy and work across all their partner institutions.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://group.sciencemuseum.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2015/11/SMG-Plan-2016-17-Plan-final-including-SM-annexe-27-07-16.pdf|title=Science Museum Group Plan 2016/17|last=Science Museum Group|access-date=2 May 2017}}{{Dead link|date=October 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}</ref> In Ireland, [[Science Gallery]] Dublin aims to increase visitors’ science capital through their exhibitions.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/science/trauma-roast-guinea-pig-and-future-of-farms-on-science-gallery-2016-menu-1.2458683|title=Trauma, roast guinea pig and future of farms on Science Gallery 2016 menu|last=Ahlstrom|first=Dick|date=8 December 2015|newspaper=The Irish Times|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref> Similarly, in the US the [[Science Museum of Minnesota]] is working with science capital to combat inequalities in access to, and participation in, science learning.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.informalscience.org/stem-justice-building-youth-science-capital|title=STEM Justice: Building Youth Science Capital|website=Informal Science|access-date=2 May 2017}}</ref>

In October 2017 the Science Capital Teaching Approach was launched at the National STEM Learning Centre in [[York]], England. The approach was co-developed and trialled over four years between Enterprising Science researchers and secondary science teachers in England.
